title: Power BI Dynamic Monthly Reports — SharePoint CSV Combine, Date Extraction, Auto-Refresh

confirmed_facts.fact: Consistent file naming enables date extraction in Power Query M. Recommended: report_YYYY_MM.csv or YYYY-MM.csv. Text.Middle extracts at zero-based offsets: 'report_' is 7 chars, year at offset 7 (4 chars), month at offset 12 (2 chars).

Adding a new CSV to the SharePoint folder automatically includes its data on the next scheduled refresh — no Power BI changes needed. SharePoint.Files() dynamically re-reads the folder listing on every refresh.

Incremental refresh for SharePoint folder: add RangeStart/RangeEnd DateTime parameters and filter files by extracted date. SharePoint doesn't support query folding, but this reduces data loaded per refresh cycle.

Power BI has no native monthly refresh schedule. Use Power Automate flow: trigger 'When a file is created in a folder' (SharePoint) → action 'Refresh a dataset' (Power BI connector) for automatic refresh on upload.

Date table can be created in DAX (CALENDAR as Calculated Table) or M (List.Dates). Must be marked as Date Table for time intelligence. DAX approach is common; M approach is preferred when the date table needs to be refreshable.

implementation_notes:
File naming extraction patterns:
```m
// report_YYYY_MM.csv — most common
FileYear  = Number.FromText(Text.Middle([Name], 7, 4))   // offset 7, 4 chars
FileMonth = Number.FromText(Text.Middle([Name], 12, 2))  // offset 12, 2 chars

// YYYY-MM.csv
Parts = Text.Split(Text.Start([Name], Text.Length([Name]) - 4), "-")
FileYear  = Number.FromText(Parts{0})
FileMonth = Number.FromText(Parts{1})

// YYYY_MM_description.csv
FileYear  = Number.FromText(Text.Start([Name], 4))
FileMonth = Number.FromText(Text.Middle([Name], 5, 2))
```

Full M query for monthly CSV combine with date extraction:
```m
let
    Source = SharePoint.Files("https://contoso.sharepoint.com/sites/MySite", [ApiVersion=15]),
    InFolder = Table.SelectRows(Source, each Text.Contains([Folder Path], "/Shared Documents/Monthly-Reports/")),
    CSVOnly = Table.SelectRows(InFolder, each [Extension] = ".csv" and not Text.StartsWith([Name], "~")),
    WithFile = Table.AddColumn(CSVOnly, "SourceFile", each [Name], type text),
    WithYear = Table.AddColumn(WithFile, "FileYear",
        each try Number.FromText(Text.Middle([SourceFile], 7, 4)) otherwise null, Int64.Type),
    WithMonth = Table.AddColumn(WithYear, "FileMonth",
        each try Number.FromText(Text.Middle([SourceFile], 12, 2)) otherwise null, Int64.Type),
    Valid = Table.SelectRows(WithMonth, each [FileYear] <> null and [FileMonth] <> null),
    Parsed = Table.AddColumn(Valid, "_Data",
        each Csv.Document([Content], [Delimiter=",", Encoding=65001, QuoteStyle=QuoteStyle.Csv])),
    Headers = Table.TransformColumns(Parsed, {"_Data", each Table.PromoteHeaders(_, [PromoteAllScalars=true])}),
    Expanded = Table.ExpandTableColumn(Headers, "_Data", Table.ColumnNames(Headers{0}[_Data])),
    WithDate = Table.AddColumn(Expanded, "MonthDate",
        each try #date([FileYear], [FileMonth], 1) otherwise null, type date),
    Cleaned = Table.RemoveColumns(WithDate,
        {"Content", "Extension", "Date accessed", "Date modified", "Date created", "Attributes", "Folder Path"})
in
    Cleaned
```

DAX date table for monthly reports:
```dax
DateTable =
VAR _Start = DATE(2023, 1, 1)
VAR _End   = TODAY()
RETURN ADDCOLUMNS(CALENDAR(_Start, _End),
    "Year",        YEAR([Date]),
    "MonthNumber", MONTH([Date]),
    "MonthName",   FORMAT([Date], "MMMM"),
    "MonthShort",  FORMAT([Date], "MMM"),
    "YearMonth",   FORMAT([Date], "YYYY-MM"),
    "Quarter",     "Q" & FORMAT(QUARTER([Date]), "0"),
    "YearQuarter", FORMAT([Date], "YYYY") & " Q" & FORMAT(QUARTER([Date]), "0"),
    "IsWeekend",   IF(WEEKDAY([Date], 2) >= 6, TRUE(), FALSE())
)
// After creating: Table Tools > Mark as date table > select Date column
```

Month slicer setup:
1. Add Slicer visual > Field = DateTable[YearMonth] (YYYY-MM format sorts correctly)
2. Style: Dropdown for compact, List for always-visible
3. For display names like 'January 2024': use MonthYear column with Sort by Column = MonthSort

Sync slicers across pages:
1. Select slicer > View > Sync slicers
2. Toggle Sync (propagate selection) and Visible (show control) per page
3. Must use same field on all synced slicers

Relative date slicer (auto-rolling):
- Field = DateTable[Date] (must be Date type, not text)
- Format > Slicer settings > Style = Relative date
- Configure: Last 12 Months

DAX measures for dynamic monthly reports:
```dax
Total Amount = SUM(FactTable[Amount])

Amount PM = CALCULATE([Total Amount], PREVIOUSMONTH('DateTable'[Date]))

MoM Change % =
    VAR _C = [Total Amount]
    VAR _P = [Amount PM]
    RETURN IF(ISBLANK(_P) || _P = 0, BLANK(), DIVIDE(_C - _P, _P))

Amount YTD = TOTALYTD([Total Amount], 'DateTable'[Date])

Amount SPLY = CALCULATE([Total Amount], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('DateTable'[Date]))

Report Title = "Monthly Report — " & SELECTEDVALUE('DateTable'[YearMonth], "All Months")
```

Steps to add a new month (zero-touch):
1. Create CSV following naming convention (e.g. report_2024_02.csv)
2. Upload to same SharePoint folder
3. Trigger refresh (manually, scheduled, or Power Automate)
4. New month's data appears in all visuals automatically
caveats: Text.Middle uses zero-based offsets — 'report_' is 7 chars, year starts at offset 7
Temp files (starting with ~) must be filtered out — they appear in SharePoint.Files results
All combined files must have matching column schemas — varying headers break ExpandTableColumn
Relative date slicer requires Date-type column (not text) — use DateTable[Date] not DateTable[YearMonth]
Syncing slicers across pages only works if both use the same table[column]
Incremental refresh for SharePoint is only beneficial with 24+ monthly files — standard full refresh is fast enough for 12-36 files
Power BI has no native 'monthly' schedule — use Power Automate for event-driven refresh on file upload
